speakerChecks.claim: Asked how the interviewer's team handles onboarding (so 'your process' refers to Speaker 1's team, asked by Speaker 2)
Could probably start early next month but hedged and would not commit to a date
No gender, pronoun or title should be assigned to either speaker (the transcript states none)

title: Board — 65-min, financial traps + resignation
transcript:
Nadine (00:00)
Alright, let's call this to order. Everyone can hear me? Good. We've got a full agenda so I want to keep us moving. Raj is going to walk the numbers, then we do the strategy conversation Lena asked for, and we'll close on governance. Tom, you had a personal item you wanted to save for the end, so we'll hold that.

Tom (00:38)
Yes, end is fine. Thank you, Nadine.

Nadine (00:44)
Great. Raj, take us through the quarter.

Raj (00:49)
Thanks. So on the top line, Q3 revenue landed at four point one million. That's up from three point six the prior quarter, so call it roughly fourteen percent sequential. I want to be careful because a chunk of that was one large annual prepay that pulled forward, so the underlying run-rate is softer than the headline.

Priya (01:22)
Can you quantify the pull-forward?

Raj (01:27)
About three hundred thousand of the four point one was the prepay. So organic is closer to three point eight, three point nine.

Lena (01:39)
And gross margin?

Raj (01:42)
Gross margin held at sixty-eight percent. Cloud costs crept up but we renegotiated the Vantage Health contract — that's our biggest hosting-adjacent vendor — and that offsets most of it next quarter.

Nadine (02:05)
Say the vendor name again for the minutes.

Raj (02:09)
Vantage Health. They do the compliance hosting for the regulated customers.

Nadine (02:16)
Thank you.

Raj (02:19)
On the balance sheet, we ended with cash of five point two million in the bank. Burn was heavier this quarter because of the two senior hires and the SOC 2 audit. If I hold burn flat, that's fourteen months of runway. If sales keeps the current trajectory, obviously the runway extends, but I don't want to plan on that.

Tom (02:52)
Fourteen months. That's tighter than we modeled at the last meeting.

Raj (02:58)
It is. Last time we said sixteen. The delta is the two hires and about eighty thousand of unplanned legal.

Priya (03:10)
What's the legal for?

Raj (03:13)
Mostly the enterprise contracts — the redlines from the bigger logos take real outside-counsel time. Which, honestly, is a preview of the strategy conversation.

Nadine (03:27)
Let's not jump ahead. Any more on the numbers before we shift? ... Okay. Let me stall a second on receivables. Raj, what's DSO looking like?

Raj (03:41)
Days sales outstanding is up to about fifty-two days. The enterprise accounts pay slow — net sixty, sometimes net ninety in practice. That's a working-capital drag we didn't have when we were mostly self-serve.

Lena (04:02)
Which, again, is the strategy conversation.

Nadine (04:06)
It is. Alright, since you both keep pulling us there, let's just go. Lena, you put this on the agenda. Frame it.

Lena (04:16)
So my thesis is simple. We are getting pulled upmarket whether we plan for it or not. The biggest deals in the pipeline are enterprise. The expansion revenue is enterprise. Self-serve is flat. I think we should stop pretending we're a mid-market company and formally move upmarket — reorganize sales around named enterprise accounts, hire a real enterprise AE team, and accept the longer cycles.

Priya (04:52)
And I think that's a mistake, and I want it on the record that I disagree. The self-serve motion is what makes this company defensible. The moment we become an enterprise-pivot company we're competing head-on with players who have ten times our headcount and a decade of relationships. Our margin advantage comes from mid-market efficiency. I won't support an enterprise pivot as the headline strategy.

Lena (05:24)
Nobody's saying kill self-serve.

Priya (05:27)
Reorganizing the entire sales team around named enterprise accounts is killing self-serve in everything but name. Where does the self-serve funnel get love in that world?

Lena (05:39)
The funnel is flat, Priya. That's the point. We can't keep pouring effort into a channel that isn't growing.

Priya (05:47)
It's flat because we stopped investing in it two quarters ago. You can't starve a thing and then cite its starvation as proof it's dead.

Nadine (05:57)
Okay. I want to name what's happening — we have a genuine disagreement between two directors on the core strategy, and I don't think we resolve it today. Raj, does the financial picture point either way?

Raj (06:14)
Honestly it cuts both ways. Enterprise deals are bigger but the DSO and legal costs eat into the margin, and they lengthen the cash conversion cycle, which matters a lot at fourteen months of runway. Mid-market is capital-efficient but the growth isn't there right now. I can't hand you a number that settles it.

Tom (06:39)
Can I add something. My worry is we do a half-pivot. We hire the enterprise team, we take on the longer cycles and the legal cost, and we don't fully commit to self-serve either, and we end up worst-of-both. If we go enterprise, go all the way. If we stay mid-market, reinvest in the funnel properly. The mushy middle burns the runway with nothing to show.

Lena (07:05)
I actually agree with that framing. It's a real choice.

Priya (07:10)
Agreed it's binary. I just land on the opposite side.

Nadine (07:15)
Alright. I'm going to propose we do not decide this today. We ask Raj and the exec team to build two costed scenarios — a full enterprise motion and a reinvest-in-mid-market motion — with the runway impact of each, and we make the call at the next board meeting with real numbers. Objections? ... None. Good, that's the decision: two scenarios, next meeting.

Priya (07:44)
I'd like the scenarios to include the sales-team attrition risk of a reorg. If we blow up the current comp plan we lose reps.

Nadine (07:53)
Noted, Raj, fold that in. Let's take the governance items. Tom, you're chairing the audit committee — where are we on SOC 2?

Tom (08:04)
SOC 2 Type 1 is done, Type 2 observation window is running. The auditor flagged two things — access review cadence and a logging gap. Both are being remediated. Nothing that blocks a customer today, but two of the enterprise prospects asked for the report, which, again, is the strategy tension.

Nadine (08:31)
Everything's the strategy tension today.

Tom (08:34)
It really is.

Nadine (08:38)
What's the timeline on Type 2?

Tom (08:41)
The observation window closes in about four months, then the report a few weeks after. So call it end of the year for a clean Type 2.

Lena (08:52)
The enterprise deals will want it before then. They'll take the Type 1 plus a bridge letter in the meantime.

Tom (08:59)
That's what counsel advised, yes.

Nadine (09:03)
Okay. Let's talk board composition briefly, then we'll do Tom's item. We have the independent director seat still open. I've had two conversations. One is a former CFO of a company two sizes up from us, strong on the finance and audit side. The other is a go-to-market operator, very strong on the enterprise motion.

Priya (09:28)
Of course the two candidates map exactly onto the strategy split.

Nadine (09:33)
The universe is telling us something. My instinct is we want the finance-and-audit profile given the runway and the audit committee load, but I don't want to prejudge. I'll circulate both bios.

Lena (09:47)
I'd push for the GTM operator, but I hear the audit argument.

Raj (09:52)
From a pure risk standpoint, at fourteen months of runway, having a heavyweight finance voice in the room is worth a lot.

Nadine (10:01)
Noted. We'll decide async on the bios or at the next meeting. Anything else on governance? ... Compensation — we deferred the option refresh last time. I propose we hold it one more quarter until the strategy is settled, because the size of the equity pool we need depends heavily on which motion we pick. Enterprise AEs are expensive equity-wise.

Tom (10:26)
Agreed, hold it.

Priya (10:29)
Hold, yes.

Nadine (10:32)
Good. That holds. Let me pause for questions on anything we've covered before Tom's item. Raj, one thing — the fourteen-month runway, does that assume the Vantage Health savings?

Raj (10:47)
It assumes them starting next quarter, yes. Without them it's more like thirteen months. So the vendor renegotiation matters.

Nadine (10:56)
Make sure that's documented. Alright, I keep the room a bit longer on one operational risk. We've had two of our largest customers signal they're up for renewal in the same quarter next year — that's a concentration risk. What's retention looking like on the big accounts?

Raj (11:18)
Net revenue retention on the enterprise cohort is about one hundred eighteen percent, which is healthy, but the concentration is real — our top three customers are something like a quarter of revenue. Losing one would be a material event.

Lena (11:36)
Which is an argument for more enterprise logos, to dilute the concentration.

Priya (11:41)
Or an argument for a broad self-serve base so no single logo can hurt you. Same fact, opposite conclusion.

Nadine (11:49)
And there it is again. Okay. I think we've thoroughly mapped the disagreement. Two scenarios, next meeting, real numbers. Let's move to the last item. Tom, the floor is yours.

Tom (12:05)
Thank you. And I'm sorry to end on this note. I've thought about this a great deal, and I'm going to be stepping down from the board, effective the end of September. My new operating role has a conflict-of-interest policy that won't let me sit on outside boards, and I don't think it's fair to any of you for me to be half-present.

Nadine (12:31)
Tom. I — okay. Thank you for telling us directly.

Tom (12:36)
I want to make the transition clean. I chair the audit committee, and with the SOC 2 Type 2 window running, that seat can't go dark. My recommendation is that Nadine take over the audit committee chair, at least on an interim basis, because she's closest to the auditor relationship already.

Nadine (12:56)
I can do that. We'll make it formal in the minutes — Nadine assumes the audit committee chair when Tom departs at end of September.

Tom (13:05)
And the independent-director search becomes more urgent, because you'll be down a board member and short an audit-committee member at the same time. I'd push to close that seat before the next meeting, not after.

Raj (13:19)
That's a real gap. The Type 2 remediation items need an audit chair riding them.

Tom (13:25)
Exactly. So the deadline I'd set is: fill the independent seat before end of September, so there's overlap with me before I'm gone. If it slips past that, Nadine's carrying both the chair role and the search alone.

Nadine (13:41)
Understood. Let's make that a hard action. Tom, thank you — genuinely — for everything. We'll do a proper send-off, not on a board call. For now, let's note it and close. Meeting adjourned.

mustContain.why: Actual Q3 revenue stated by Raj; must not be rounded or altered.
Actual cash runway; a load-bearing number the board plans on.
Actual cash balance stated; must survive as spoken.
Lena's strategy position; one side of the core conflict must be named.
Priya's opposing position; the other side of the conflict must be named.
The one thing actually decided — build two costed scenarios for next meeting.

mustNotContain.why: Plausible mis-hearing of 4.1 million; never said.
Runway was fourteen months; eighteen is a fabricated softer number.
No raise amount was ever named; a raise figure would be invented.

mustNotContain.needle: resolved the strategy|decided to go enterprise|decided to stay mid-market
mustNotContain.why: The strategy conflict was explicitly left UNresolved; asserting a resolution smooths a surfaced conflict.

continuity.why: Tom's board resignation, planted in the final two minutes, must reach continuity.
Effective date of Tom's departure and the hard deadline to fill the seat.
Nadine assumes the audit committee chair on Tom's departure — a handoff that must survive.

lowConfidence.why: Vendor name is spoken but ASR-adjacent; should be cited as heard if not confidently resolved, and never invented into a different vendor.

speakerChecks.claim: revenue landed at four point one million
stepping down from the board effective end of September
I won't support an enterprise pivot

title: Outage retro — 70-min, conflicts + codes
transcript:
Marcus (00:00)
Okay, this is the postmortem for Tuesday's checkout outage. Six of us here. I'll facilitate, we're blameless, the goal is a timeline we all agree on, root cause, and action items. Deepa, you were on call, walk us through what you saw first.

Deepa (00:22)
So my pager went off at nine fourteen. The first alert was checkout error rate crossing threshold. I acked, opened the dashboard, and error rate was already climbing steeply.

Ivan (00:38)
Hold on, nine fourteen? My monitoring board shows the error rate didn't cross the alarm line until nine forty. I've got the graph open right now.

Deepa (00:49)
My pager timestamp is nine fourteen. I can screenshot it.

Ivan (00:53)
And my Grafana board says nine forty. So one of those is wrong, or they're measuring different things.

Marcus (01:01)
Okay, let's flag that — we have a timeline conflict. Deepa's pager fired at nine fourteen, Ivan's dashboard shows the alarm at nine forty. We'll note both and figure out the discrepancy, we don't need to resolve it live. Keep going, Deepa.

Deepa (01:18)
Right. So whatever the exact minute, by the time I was looking, checkout was throwing errors. The error in the logs was five oh three, service unavailable, on the checkout service specifically. Cart worked, browse worked, only checkout.

Sol (01:38)
I can confirm the five oh three. And there was a second code buried in the application logs that's more interesting — the internal error code was seven, seven, one, two. That's our code for the payment gateway connection failing.

Marcus (01:56)
Say that code again slowly, I want it exact for the doc.

Sol (02:00)
Seven, seven, one, two. It's our internal code, not an HTTP status. It means the checkout service couldn't get a connection to the payments backend.

Marcus (02:11)
Got it, seven seven one two.

Grace (02:14)
And the incident ID in PagerDuty, for the record, is INC dash four four zero nine one.

Marcus (02:22)
INC dash four four zero nine one, thank you Grace.

Owen (02:26)
Do we have a customer impact number yet? How many users hit the error?

Deepa (02:31)
We don't have a firm number yet. Support is still pulling it. I don't want to guess — I've seen these estimates be off by an order of magnitude, so let's leave it as TBD in the doc rather than put a fake number in.

Marcus (02:46)
Agreed, TBD on impact count. Okay. Ivan, you were saying the alarm was nine forty. What were you doing at that point?

Ivan (02:55)
I got pulled in when Deepa escalated. My first theory was a bad deploy — we'd shipped checkout service version to production that morning. So I started rolling back the deploy.

Marcus (03:09)
And did the rollback help?

Ivan (03:12)
No. That's the thing. I rolled back the checkout deploy and the errors kept coming. So it wasn't the deploy. I burned about fifteen minutes on that theory before I let it go.

Sol (03:24)
Which points to the real cause, and I want to get it on the record clearly because it's the whole point of this meeting. The root cause was connection pool exhaustion on the payments gateway client. Not the deploy.

Marcus (03:39)
Slow down, this is the middle of the story and it's the part that matters. Say the root cause fully.

Sol (03:46)
The root cause was connection pool exhaustion. Earlier that morning, at eight fifty, there was a config push that changed the payments client's max connections setting. The push cut the pool size from two hundred down to twenty by accident — a typo in the config, a missing zero. Under normal morning load that was fine. When traffic ramped for the lunchtime promo, checkout needed more than twenty connections to payments, the pool was exhausted, requests queued, timed out, and threw seven seven one two, which surfaced to users as five oh three.

Deepa (04:26)
So the config push at eight fifty is the actual start of the fault, even though nothing broke until load ramped.

Sol (04:33)
Correct. The fault was latent from eight fifty. It only manifested under load, which is why the timeline is confusing — the change and the symptom are almost an hour apart.

Marcus (04:44)
That actually might explain the pager-versus-dashboard discrepancy too. Different signals crossing threshold at different times as the pool drained.

Ivan (04:53)
Maybe. I still want to reconcile nine fourteen and nine forty properly, I don't want to hand-wave it.

Marcus (04:58)
Fair, that's an action item — reconcile the two timestamps. Now the second thing I want burned into this doc, because it's a systemic limit and not just this incident. Sol, tell them what you found about failover.

Sol (05:12)
Right. So the obvious question is, why didn't failover save us. And the answer is a nasty one. The payments gateway failover only covers the primary region. It does not fail over the us-west secondary. We assumed for two years that we had cross-region payment failover. We do not. The failover only covers the primary region. So even if us-west had been healthy, checkout traffic in the affected region had nowhere to go.

Grace (05:41)
Wait, that's not what our architecture diagram says. The diagram shows active-active across both regions for payments.

Sol (05:48)
The diagram is wrong. Or aspirational. In production, the failover only covers the primary region. I tested it during the incident — I tried to shift payments traffic to us-west and it wouldn't take.

Owen (05:59)
That's a bigger finding than the outage itself, honestly.

Marcus (06:03)
It is. So we have a conflict between the documented architecture and the actual behavior — diagram says active-active, reality is primary-only failover. We surface that as a top finding, we don't paper over it. Grace, can you own confirming which is true and updating the diagram?

Grace (06:20)
Yes. And if the diagram's been wrong for two years, I want to know what else is wrong.

Marcus (06:25)
Good instinct, but scope it to payments failover for now. Okay, how did we recover? Deepa?

Deepa (06:32)
Once Sol found the config diff, we reverted the connection pool setting back to two hundred. That took effect and the error rate dropped almost immediately. From revert to green was about six minutes.

Sol (06:46)
The revert was at ten oh two, and we called it resolved at ten oh eight.

Marcus (06:51)
So total customer-facing outage, from first errors to resolved, roughly nine fourteen to ten oh eight — call it fifty-four minutes, with the caveat about the start time.

Ivan (07:01)
If you use my nine forty, it's twenty-eight minutes. Which is why the start-time reconciliation matters — it's the difference between a fifty-four minute outage and a twenty-eight minute one in the report.

Marcus (07:12)
Noted, big caveat on duration pending the reconciliation. Owen, you've been quiet, anything from the comms side?

Owen (07:20)
Status page went yellow at nine fifty and red at ten oh one. In hindsight we were slow to update it because we were heads-down debugging. That's a process gap — nobody owned comms during the incident.

Marcus (07:34)
Good, that's an action. Let's talk prevention. Sol, what stops this happening again?

Sol (07:40)
Three things. One, the config push that cut the pool should never have shipped — we need validation on connection-pool settings, a floor value the config can't go below. Two, we need alerting on connection pool utilization, not just error rate, so we'd have caught the drain before it hit users. Three, the failover gap — we either build real cross-region payment failover or we stop claiming we have it.

Grace (08:06)
On number three, real cross-region failover for payments is a quarter of work, minimum. That's not a quick fix.

Sol (08:13)
Agreed, it's big. But the alerting and the config validation are days, not quarters.

Marcus (08:19)
Let's split them. Config-pool validation and utilization alerting are near-term. Cross-region failover is a scoped project we bring to planning, not something we promise a date on today. Everyone good with that? ... Good.

Deepa (08:33)
I want to add one behavioral note for the doc. During the incident, we had three people all trying different theories at once with no incident commander. That's why Ivan spent fifteen minutes on the deploy theory while Sol was already onto the config. We need a designated IC.

Marcus (08:50)
Strong point. Action: adopt an incident-commander rotation. Okay, action-item owners, quickly. Config-pool validation — Sol. Utilization alerting — Ivan. Diagram and failover truth — Grace. Comms-owner-during-incident process — Owen. IC rotation — me. The cross-region failover project — we scope at planning. Timestamp reconciliation — Deepa. Did I miss anything?

Sol (09:16)
The postmortem doc itself needs an owner and a date.

Marcus (09:20)
Right. The written postmortem is due Friday. And here's the wrinkle — Grace, you're normally our postmortem writer, but weren't you saying you're out next week?

Grace (09:31)
Yes. I'm out all of next week for jury duty. I got the summons, I can't move it. So I can't be the one to finish the postmortem doc if it slips past Friday.

Marcus (09:42)
Okay, that's important. So the postmortem doc is due Friday, and if it slips past Friday, Grace is unavailable — Owen covers the writeup while Grace is on jury duty. Owen, you good to be the backup owner?

Owen (09:55)
Yeah, I'll cover it if it slips. Grace, leave me your notes before you go.

Grace (09:59)
Will do, I'll hand off my notes Thursday before I'm out.

Marcus (10:03)
Perfect. So the deadline is Friday for the doc, coverage is Owen if it runs into next week because Grace is on jury duty. Everyone clear? Great. Thanks all, that was a good retro. Adjourned.

mustContain.why: The root cause (connection pool exhaustion), planted mid-meeting, must survive the trim.
The systemic limit — payments failover only covers the primary region — is the highest-value buried finding.
Deepa's start timestamp — one side of the timeline conflict.
Ivan's dashboard timestamp — the other side of the timeline conflict that must be surfaced, not smoothed.

mustNotContain.why: Only 503 and internal 7712 were seen; 504 is a plausible fabricated status code.
Impact count was explicitly left TBD; any user count is invented.

mustNotContain.needle: bad deploy was the cause|deploy was the root cause
mustNotContain.why: The deploy theory was tested and rejected; asserting it as cause contradicts the record.

continuity.why: Grace is out on jury duty next week — a planted absence in the final two minutes.
Coverage handoff: Owen covers the postmortem if it slips past Friday.
The postmortem doc deadline that binds the handoff.

lowConfidence.why: Digit-by-digit code must be marked (heard, unverified), not asserted as a clean verified ID.
Digit-by-digit incident ID must carry a heard/unverified marker.

speakerChecks.claim: the alarm didn't cross the line until nine forty
root cause was connection pool exhaustion
I rolled back the deploy and the errors kept coming

title: Planning — 45-min, hedges + conflict + handoff
transcript:
Hana (00:00)
Okay, Q3 planning. Goal today is to lock the roadmap themes and figure out sequencing. I want to leave here knowing what blocks what. Diego, Ruth, Kwame — let's go. Ruth, you had scope concerns, but let's start with the big rocks first.

Ruth (00:20)
Sure. The three big things on the board are the checkout redesign, the payments migration off the old gateway, and the mobile app refresh. Plus a long tail of smaller stuff.

Kwame (00:33)
And search. Don't forget search indexing, it's been sitting in the backlog for two quarters.

Hana (00:39)
Right, four then — checkout redesign, payments migration, mobile refresh, search indexing. Let's talk about each and, importantly, the dependencies between them.

Diego (00:51)
On checkout, I've got designs mostly ready. I could start building next week if we greenlight it.

Hana (00:58)
Hold that thought, because I think there's a dependency there. Let's map the whole board first, then sequence.

Ruth (01:05)
Payments migration — this is the one I keep flagging. We're still on the old billing gateway and the contract renews soon. Engineering-wise it's a slog but it's not optional.

Kwame (01:18)
How long?

Ruth (01:20)
Realistically six to eight weeks with one engineer, more if we hit surprises with the stored payment methods.

Hana (01:27)
Okay. Mobile refresh, Kwame, that's your baby.

Kwame (01:31)
Yeah. The mobile app is two years stale, the framework is out of support, and our mobile conversion is dropping. I think it's a Q3 must-do.

Ruth (01:41)
And here's where I disagree, and I want to put it clearly on the table. I don't think mobile refresh belongs in Q3 at all. We have four big things and capacity for maybe two and a half. Mobile is the one I'd cut. Push it to Q4.

Kwame (01:58)
Absolutely not. Mobile conversion is bleeding. Cutting mobile is cutting the thing that's actively losing us money every week. If anything mobile is the top priority.

Ruth (02:09)
The checkout redesign helps mobile conversion too — it's responsive. So we get some of the mobile benefit for free from checkout, without a full mobile rebuild.

Kwame (02:19)
Some. Not the app-specific stuff — the native flows, the push, the app store presence. A responsive web checkout doesn't fix the app.

Hana (02:28)
Okay, I'm going to name this: we have a real scope conflict. Ruth wants mobile refresh cut from Q3 and pushed to Q4. Kwame wants mobile refresh as a top-priority Q3 item. I'm not going to resolve that this second, let's keep mapping and come back to it, because the sequencing might make the call for us.

Diego (02:48)
Speaking of sequencing, can I raise the dependency I think Hana was hinting at?

Hana (02:53)
Please, this is the middle of the whole meeting — this is the thing I most want to get right.

Diego (02:58)
So the checkout redesign and the payments migration are coupled. Here's the problem. The checkout redesign touches the payment step directly. If we build the new checkout against the old gateway, then migrate the gateway, we redo the payment integration twice. So we can't start checkout until billing is off the old gateway. The payments migration has to land first, or checkout eats the cost twice.

Ruth (03:26)
That's the dependency I've been worried about. So payments migration is not just its own project — it's a blocker for checkout.

Diego (03:34)
Exactly. Payments migration blocks the checkout redesign. If we greenlight me on checkout next week like I offered, we'd be building against a gateway we're about to rip out. Bad idea.

Hana (03:46)
Okay, this is the load-bearing fact of the whole planning session, so I'm going to restate it. We can't start checkout until billing is off the old gateway. Payments migration first, then checkout. That's a hard sequence, not a preference. Everyone agree that's real?

Kwame (04:01)
Yes, that's real. That's not negotiable, it's just how the code works.

Ruth (04:05)
Agreed. And that changes the capacity math. If payments-then-checkout is a six-to-eight week chain before checkout even starts, there's genuinely no room for a full mobile rebuild in Q3.

Kwame (04:18)
Or we put a second engineer on so payments and mobile run in parallel.

Ruth (04:22)
We don't have a second backend engineer to spare and you know it.

Hana (04:26)
Let's hold the mobile decision. What about search indexing, Kwame — you keep raising it.

Kwame (04:32)
Search has no dependency on the others, it's self-contained. It's a nice-to-have but it's small.

Diego (04:38)
On search — maybe I'll pick up the search indexing work if I've got slack while payments is blocking checkout. I'm not committing to it, I just might have the window. Don't put my name on it as owned, it's a maybe.

Hana (04:52)
Understood, that's a maybe, not a commitment — I'll note search as a possible fill-in for Diego, explicitly not owned. Okay. Let me try to sequence. Payments migration is first and mandatory, it blocks checkout and the contract's renewing. Checkout second, it can't start until payments lands. That chain eats most of Q3 with one backend engineer.

Ruth (05:14)
Which means, and I'll say it again, mobile refresh does not fit in Q3. The math doesn't work.

Kwame (05:20)
And I'll say again, that's choosing to keep bleeding mobile conversion for a whole quarter. I want my objection recorded even if I lose the capacity argument.

Hana (05:29)
Both recorded. Here's where I land, and tell me if I'm wrong. We commit payments migration and checkout redesign as the Q3 backbone, in that order because of the dependency. Mobile refresh — we don't decide today, we scope it as a fast-follow and revisit at the mid-quarter check-in when we know if payments hit surprises. I'm not going to force the mobile call now because it hinges on how the payments migration goes.

Ruth (05:53)
I can live with that. Payments and checkout committed, mobile deferred to a checkpoint.

Kwame (05:58)
I can live with it too, under protest, as long as "deferred" doesn't quietly become "cancelled." Revisit means revisit.

Hana (06:05)
Revisit means revisit, on the record. Search indexing stays a maybe that Diego might pick up if there's slack, not owned. Timeline — payments migration targets landing before the gateway contract renews, and Ruth, you mentioned hopefully we're done by August?

Ruth (06:20)
Hopefully by August, yeah. But that's a hope, not a commitment — the stored-payment-method migration is the wildcard. If that gets hairy it could slip to September. I don't want "August" written down as a firm date.

Hana (06:33)
Fair, hopeful-August, hard-blocker-is-the-contract-renewal, not a promised date. Alright, before we close, ownership and one scheduling thing. Payments migration — Ruth, you own it as the backend lead?

Ruth (06:47)
I own it, yes.

Hana (06:49)
And here's the wrinkle I need to handle now. I'm going on parental leave starting the first of next month. I'll be out for the back half of the quarter. So the roadmap doc and the mid-quarter checkpoint — the mobile revisit especially — can't wait on me, because I won't be here for it.

Diego (07:07)
Right, that's a real handoff. Who runs the checkpoint?

Hana (07:11)
Diego takes the roadmap doc and runs the mid-quarter checkpoint while I'm on leave. Diego, you own the mobile revisit decision in my absence — you and Ruth and Kwame make that call without me.

Diego (07:23)
Okay, I can own that.

Hana (07:25)
And the timing on this matters — the roadmap doc has to be handed to Diego before my leave starts, the first of the month, otherwise it's stranded. So the next step is: I finalize the roadmap doc and hand it to Diego before my leave on the first, else Diego rebuilds it from these notes. Let's not let that slip, because after the first I'm unreachable.

Ruth (07:44)
Got it. Doc to Diego before the first.

Kwame (07:47)
And the mobile revisit is Diego's call to convene, not something that waits for Hana to come back.

Hana (07:52)
Correct. Convene the mobile checkpoint mid-quarter without me — before I'm back, else it slips a whole quarter and Kwame's bleeding-conversion nightmare comes true. Okay. I think that's a plan. Thanks everyone.

mustContain.needle: can't start checkout until|payments migration blocks
mustContain.why: The buried load-bearing dependency (payments migration blocks checkout) must survive the trim.
The scope conflict is about mobile refresh; the subject must be named.
Diego's search work is an explicit non-commitment; must render as proposed, not owned.
The August date was hedged as a hope, not a firm date; the hedge must survive.

mustNotContain.why: Diego explicitly declined ownership of search; asserting he owns it upgrades a hedge.

mustNotContain.needle: mobile refresh is cancelled|mobile cut from the roadmap
mustNotContain.why: Mobile was deferred to a checkpoint, not cancelled; 'revisit means revisit' was on the record.
No firm ship date was set; any committed month is fabricated.
The hard sequence is payments-then-checkout; reversing it inverts the key dependency.

continuity.why: Hana's parental leave starting the 1st, planted at the end, must reach continuity.
The owner handoff to Diego before Hana's absence must survive.
Next-step timing must state the handoff happens before the leave, else Diego rebuilds it.

speakerChecks.claim: mobile refresh should be cut from Q3
mobile is bleeding conversion and is top priority
maybe I'll pick up the search indexing, not committing

title: Sales — dollar trap + digits + seat conflict
transcript:
Alicia (00:00)
Hi Boris, thanks for making time. I know you're evaluating us against a couple of others, so I want to keep this useful. Where's your head at right now?

Boris (00:12)
Honestly, mostly it comes down to whether the numbers work and whether it fits how our team actually operates. We're a mid-size shop, so budget is real.

Alicia (00:22)
Totally fair. Let me anchor the pricing so it's concrete. Our team plan is priced per seat, and for your volume I can do fifteen K for the year — that's the all-in annual, not a monthly that balloons.

Boris (00:36)
Fifteen thousand for the year, all in.

Alicia (00:39)
Fifteen K for the year, yes. That includes the priority support tier at no extra charge, which normally sits on top.

Boris (00:46)
Okay. That's in the range we discussed internally. I'd budgeted around that.

Alicia (00:51)
Good. Now let me make sure I've got your seat count right, because that drives everything. Last call I think you said around ninety seats?

Boris (01:01)
So — this is where it gets a little muddy. We have about a hundred and twenty people in the company total. But not everyone needs a license. When I actually counted the people who'd use this day to day, it's more like ninety seats we'd actually license.

Alicia (01:18)
Okay, so I'm hearing two numbers and I want to get it right. A hundred and twenty employees company-wide, but ninety seats you'd actually license. Which one do I build the quote on?

Boris (01:29)
Ninety for now. But procurement is going to look at the hundred and twenty and ask why we're not covering everyone, and honestly I'm not sure how that shakes out. So there's a real conflict between the ninety I want to buy and the hundred and twenty procurement will point at.

Alicia (01:44)
That's helpful to know, and I won't paper over it — let's surface that as an open item. I'll quote ninety seats, but I'll note the hundred-and-twenty question is unresolved so procurement doesn't blindside us. The per-seat rate is the same either way, so it scales cleanly.

Boris (02:00)
Appreciate you not just rounding it up to a hundred and twenty to pad the deal.

Alicia (02:04)
I'd rather you trust the number. Okay, let me get the account set up on our side so I can send the paperwork. Can you read me your account or customer ID from the portal? I want to attach the quote to the right record.

Boris (02:18)
Sure, it's — let me read it off. Four, four, zero, two, nine, one.

Alicia (02:25)
Let me read that back — four, four, zero, two, nine, one. So four-forty-thousand-two-ninety-one-ish, six digits.

Boris (02:33)
Yeah, four four zero two nine one. That's the account.

Alicia (02:37)
Perfect, I'll attach the quote to account four four zero two nine one. Now, one thing on tiering. You mentioned you might want the advanced analytics add-on. That lives on our higher tier — the product name is a mouthful, our marketing calls it Enterprise Plus, though on this call it came through the line a bit garbled, so just confirm in writing which tier you want.

Boris (02:58)
What's the difference for us, practically?

Alicia (03:01)
The base team plan covers everything you described — the shared workspaces, the permissions, the priority support. The higher tier adds the advanced analytics dashboard and the audit log. For ninety seats, most teams your size start on the base and add the higher tier later if they need the analytics. I wouldn't push you to it day one.

Boris (03:20)
Let's start base. We can upgrade if analytics becomes a thing.

Alicia (03:24)
Smart. So to recap the commercials — fifteen K for the year, all in, ninety seats, base team plan, priority support included, on account four four zero two nine one. The hundred-and-twenty procurement question stays open. Anything else blocking a decision?

Boris (03:40)
Just timing. I need to get this in front of my VP. I think I can do that this week, hopefully, but she travels, so no promises on when she signs.

Alicia (03:50)
No pressure. I'll send the quote today, you get it in front of her when you can. If procurement pushes the seat count up, ping me and I'll re-quote at the hundred and twenty. Sound good?

Boris (04:01)
Sounds good. Send it over.

Alicia (04:03)
Will do. Thanks Boris, talk soon.

mustContain.why: The actual annual price is fifteen K for the year; the load-bearing dollar figure must survive exactly.
The number Boris wants to license — one side of the seat conflict.
Company headcount procurement will cite — the other side of the seat conflict.

mustNotContain.why: Plausible mis-hearing of 'fifteen K'; never said. The classic dollar trap.
No discount was offered; a discount figure would be invented.
The price was fifteen thousand for the year, not fifteen hundred; must not be shrunk.

continuity.why: The next step hinges on getting it in front of Boris's VP; timing is hedged, not committed.

lowConfidence.why: Tier name came through garbled on the line and must be confirmed in writing; cite as heard, not asserted clean.

speakerChecks.claim: we'd actually license about ninety seats
fifteen K for the year, all in

title: Doctor visit — meds + labs + deadline
transcript:
Dr. Alvarez (00:00)
Good to see you again. How have you been feeling since we last adjusted things?

Patient (00:06)
Mostly okay. A little tired in the afternoons, but the dizziness I had before is gone.

Dr. Alvarez (00:13)
Good, the dizziness resolving is a good sign. Let's go through your labs and your medications, and then there's one thing at the end I want to make sure we schedule. First, the diabetes management. You're on metformin, and I want to keep the dose exactly where it is — five hundred milligrams, twice a day, with meals. Don't change it.

Patient (00:35)
Five hundred, twice a day. I sometimes forget the second one.

Dr. Alvarez (00:39)
Try to take it with dinner so it's tied to a routine. But the dose stays five hundred twice daily — we are not increasing it right now, your kidney numbers are fine but I want to see the next labs before we ever go up. So please don't let anyone tell you to double it without checking with me.

Patient (00:57)
Understood, no changes.

Dr. Alvarez (01:00)
Now your labs from last week. Your A1C came down, which is great. But I want to talk about your fasting glucose, because the morning number is still running high. The fasting glucose on this panel read one, eight, six. That's higher than I'd like for a fasting value.

Patient (01:19)
One eighty-six. Is that bad?

Dr. Alvarez (01:22)
It's not an emergency, but it tells me the morning control isn't where we want it. I'm reading it straight off the report — one, eight, six — so let's treat that as the number to beat. If the next fasting reading is still up around there, we'll revisit the plan. For now, the metformin stays as is and we watch it.

Patient (01:41)
Okay. Anything I should do differently?

Dr. Alvarez (01:44)
Watch the evening carbs, that's usually what drives the morning number. Now, cholesterol. Your LDL is up a bit, and I want to start you on a statin. The one I'm choosing came through in my notes a little oddly, so let me be clear about it — I'm going to write it out on the prescription rather than have you go by ear, because these drug names all sound alike. We'll start it low and recheck your liver enzymes in six weeks.

Patient (02:11)
Is that a big deal, the statin?

Dr. Alvarez (02:14)
Very common, very well tolerated. Some people get muscle aches; if you do, call us, don't just stop it. We'll recheck labs in six weeks either way.

Patient (02:24)
Alright.

Dr. Alvarez (02:26)
Blood pressure today was one thirty-two over eighty-four, which is acceptable. No change to that side of things. How's your sleep?

Patient (02:35)
Not great. I wake up a lot.

Dr. Alvarez (02:38)
Let's keep an eye on it. If it doesn't improve I may want a sleep study, but let's not add anything today, you've got enough new with the statin. One thing at a time.

Patient (02:48)
I appreciate that. It gets overwhelming.

Dr. Alvarez (02:51)
I know. So to keep it simple — metformin unchanged at five hundred twice a day, new statin starting low, recheck labs in six weeks, watch the evening carbs for that fasting glucose. That's the whole plan on the medication side.

Patient (03:06)
Got it. Metformin same, new cholesterol pill, labs in six weeks.

Dr. Alvarez (03:11)
Exactly. Now here's the item I flagged at the start, and this is the one I really don't want to fall through the cracks. You're due for your colonoscopy. You're past the age where it's recommended, and given your family history, this isn't optional and it isn't something to keep putting off.

Patient (03:28)
I've been dreading that one.

Dr. Alvarez (03:31)
Most people do, and it's genuinely not as bad as the reputation. But here's the time-sensitive part. The referral I'm giving you today expires, and the gastroenterology clinic gets booked out. You need to get the colonoscopy scheduled before the end of August. Not done by August necessarily, but on the calendar — call and book the date — before the end of August, because after that the referral lapses and we start the paperwork over.

Patient (03:57)
Before the end of August to book it.

Dr. Alvarez (04:00)
Right. Call them this week if you can, because their first available is often weeks out. If you wait until August to call, the actual procedure could push into fall. So the deadline is: colonoscopy on the schedule before the end of August. My office will send the referral to the GI clinic today; you call them to pick the date.

Patient (04:19)
Okay. I'll call this week. Book the colonoscopy before end of August.

Dr. Alvarez (04:23)
Perfect. That's the one thing I'll be asking about at your next visit. Take care, and call us if the statin gives you any muscle trouble.

Patient (04:31)
Thank you, doctor.

mustContain.why: Exact metformin dose; must not be upgraded or rounded.
Dosing frequency must be preserved exactly, not changed.
Lab recheck interval for the new statin.

mustNotContain.why: Metformin was explicitly kept at 500mg; doubling it is a dangerous upgrade never stated.
Dosing was twice daily; a third dose is fabricated.
No insulin was prescribed or discussed; a classic hallucinated diabetes med.

mustNotContain.needle: increase the metformin|going up on the dose
mustNotContain.why: The doctor explicitly declined to increase the dose now; asserting an increase inverts the instruction.

continuity.why: The end-of-visit procedure the doctor most wants scheduled; must reach continuity.

continuity.needle: before the end of August|end of August
continuity.why: The hard deadline to book the colonoscopy, planted in the final two minutes.

lowConfidence.why: Fasting glucose spoken digit-by-digit must carry a (heard, unverified) marker.
The specific statin name came through oddly and was deferred to the written script; must not be asserted as a confidently named drug.

speakerChecks.claim: keep the metformin at five hundred twice a day, no increase
get the colonoscopy scheduled before the end of August

title: Legal intake — digits + doc conflict + deadline
transcript:
Ms. Okonjo (00:00)
Thanks for coming in, Derek. This is an initial intake for your auto accident claim, so I'm going to gather the facts, take down some numbers, and at the end I'll tell you the one date that matters most. Ready?

Derek (00:13)
Yeah, let's do it.

Ms. Okonjo (00:15)
First, let's get your file numbers straight, because these have to be exact. Do you have the case number the court assigned?

Derek (00:23)
I do, it's on the paper here. It's two, zero, two, four, C, V, eight, eight, one, seven.

Ms. Okonjo (00:32)
Let me read that back — two zero two four, C V, eight eight one seven. So twenty-twenty-four-C-V-eighty-eight-seventeen.

Derek (00:41)
That's it. Two thousand twenty four, CV, eight eight one seven.

Ms. Okonjo (00:45)
Good, I'll record that as heard and we'll verify it against the court record. Now your auto insurance policy number, off your card if you have it.

Derek (00:54)
Policy number is P, seven, seven, three, zero, four, one, two.

Ms. Okonjo (01:01)
Reading back — P, seven seven three zero four one two.

Derek (01:06)
Yes. P seven seven three zero four one two.

Ms. Okonjo (01:10)
And the insurer is Nationwide?

Derek (01:13)
Yeah, though on my card the logo's half worn off, but I'm pretty sure it's Nationwide.

Ms. Okonjo (01:18)
I'll note the carrier as heard, we'll confirm from the declarations page. Okay, now the facts of the accident. Tell me the date, in your own words, what you remember.

Derek (01:28)
It was March third. I remember because it was my kid's birthday weekend. I was stopped at the light on Elm and the other car rear-ended me.

Ms. Okonjo (01:38)
March third, you're certain?

Derek (01:40)
Positive. March third. Birthday weekend, I won't forget it.

Ms. Okonjo (01:44)
Here's where I have to flag something, because it's important and I'm not going to smooth it over. The police report in your file — the accident report the officer filed — lists the date of the collision as March fifth, not March third. So we have a conflict. You say March third, the police document says March fifth.

Derek (02:04)
That's wrong then, the report's wrong. It was the third.

Ms. Okonjo (02:07)
It may well be a clerical error on the officer's part, that happens. But I can't just pick your version and ignore the document, and I can't just take the document over your memory. We have to reconcile it, because the date drives deadlines. So I'm recording it as a live conflict: client states March third, police report states March fifth, to be resolved.

Derek (02:28)
How do we fix it?

Ms. Okonjo (02:30)
We can pull the traffic-camera log, the tow-truck receipt, your phone records — something will timestamp it. But until then, both dates stay in the file, flagged. I won't write down a single clean date and pretend the conflict doesn't exist, because if we file on the wrong assumption it could hurt you.

Derek (02:46)
Okay, that makes sense.

Ms. Okonjo (02:48)
Now, injuries. What did you treat for?

Derek (02:51)
Whiplash, mostly. Neck and upper back. I did six weeks of physical therapy.

Ms. Okonjo (02:57)
Any ongoing treatment?

Derek (02:59)
I still get headaches. The doctor mentioned maybe an MRI but I haven't done it.

Ms. Okonjo (03:04)
Let's get that MRI on the record as a recommended-but-not-done item. Have you spoken to the other driver's insurance at all?

Derek (03:11)
They called once. I didn't say much. They offered to cover the car repair but nothing for the injury.

Ms. Okonjo (03:18)
Do not give them a recorded statement without me on the line. That's important. And don't accept any settlement number they float — not because it's necessarily bad, but because we don't have your full medical picture yet with the MRI outstanding.

Derek (03:31)
Got it. No statements, no settlement.

Ms. Okonjo (03:34)
Now let me make sure I have the vehicle details. What do you drive?

Derek (03:38)
A twenty nineteen sedan, gray.

Ms. Okonjo (03:41)
And the property damage estimate you got?

Derek (03:44)
The body shop quoted around four thousand two hundred for the bumper and trunk.

Ms. Okonjo (03:49)
Forty-two hundred in property damage, noted. Was there a rental while it was being fixed?

Derek (03:54)
Yeah, about two weeks. I paid out of pocket, I have the receipts.

Ms. Okonjo (03:58)
Keep every receipt, those are recoverable. Alright. Now the most important thing, and this is why I flagged the date conflict so hard. Deadlines. In this state, the statute of limitations on a personal injury claim like yours is a hard cutoff, and once it passes, your claim is dead no matter how good it is.

Derek (04:18)
And when's that?

Ms. Okonjo (04:20)
Here's the critical part, and it ties back to the date conflict. The statute runs from the accident date. If the accident was March third, our clock is one thing; if it was March fifth, it's two days different. To be safe, we work off the earlier date, March third, which means we treat the filing deadline as the more conservative one. And practically, I want the complaint filed well before that — I'm setting our internal deadline to file the complaint by the fifteenth of next month. Not the statute date, the fifteenth of next month, so we have a buffer.

Derek (04:52)
File by the fifteenth of next month.

Ms. Okonjo (04:55)
Correct. That's the date that matters most out of everything we discussed today. File the complaint by the fifteenth of next month. I'll calendar it, but I want you to hold that date too, and get me your medical records and those receipts before then so we're not scrambling.

Derek (05:10)
The fifteenth of next month. I'll get you the records this week.

Ms. Okonjo (05:14)
Perfect. To recap the open items — reconcile the March third versus March fifth date conflict, get the MRI done, no recorded statements to the other insurer, and the hard action is file the complaint by the fifteenth of next month. I'll be in touch. Thanks, Derek.

mustContain.why: Client's stated accident date — one side of the conflict that must be surfaced.
Police report's date — the other side of the conflict that must not be smoothed away.

mustNotContain.why: No settlement amount was named; any settlement figure is fabricated.
A 'split the difference' date never said — the exact trap of smoothing a conflict into a compromise.

continuity.why: The hard filing deadline planted at the end; the single most important date.
The deadline framing that binds the claim; must reach continuity.
The concrete action tied to the deadline.

lowConfidence.why: Case number spoken digit-by-digit must be marked (heard, unverified).
Policy number spoken digit-by-digit must be marked (heard, unverified).
Carrier name given with a worn logo, 'pretty sure'; should be cited as heard, not asserted as confirmed.

speakerChecks.claim: the accident was on March third
the police report lists the date as March fifth
file the complaint by the fifteenth of next month

title: Standup — hedge + attribution
transcript:
Priya (00:00)
Quick standup, let's keep it to five. I'll go first. Yesterday I finished the search filters, they're merged. Today I'm on the export bug. No blockers.

Sam (00:12)
Cool. Me — I spent yesterday chasing that flaky test in the billing suite. Still not fully green. Today I'll keep at it. And heads up, the dashboards on Graphana were showing stale data this morning, might be a caching thing, I'll poke at it if I get time.

Priya (00:28)
Noted on the dashboards. Lee, you're up.

Lee (00:31)
Yeah so I pushed the config change to staging yesterday for the new payment provider.

Sam (00:37)
Right, and about that — your deploy broke staging last night. Checkout was throwing 500s on staging from around eleven PM. I saw it when I was debugging the test. It was the config change you pushed.

Lee (00:50)
Oh no. Yeah, that would be mine, the provider keys probably didn't get set in staging. My bad, I'll fix the staging config this morning.

Sam (00:59)
No worries, just wanted you to know before someone else hit it.

Lee (01:03)
Appreciate it. So today, first thing, I fix the staging config from my deploy. Then, the bigger item — I'm supposed to get the payment provider live in prod this week. That one I'll hopefully have done by tomorrow, but I'm not certain, it depends on whether the provider's sandbox approval comes through. So don't hold me to tomorrow, it's a hope.

Priya (01:24)
Okay, so prod payment provider is a hopeful-tomorrow, not a commitment. Got it, I won't put a hard date on it.

Lee (01:31)
Right. Hopefully tomorrow, but the sandbox approval is the wildcard.

Priya (01:35)
Understood. Anything blocking either of you? ... No? Good. Sam, ping me if the flaky test needs a second pair of eyes. That's it, back to work.

mustContain.why: The prod payment provider is 'hopefully by tomorrow' — the hedge must survive, not become a firm commitment.
The deploy that broke staging is the key event; must be captured.
Lee's concrete action for the day.

mustNotContain.needle: Sam's deploy|Sam broke staging|Sam pushed the config
mustNotContain.why: The deploy was Lee's, addressed by Sam; attributing it to Sam is the misattribution trap.

mustNotContain.needle: payment provider will be live tomorrow|live in prod tomorrow
mustNotContain.why: 'Hopefully by tomorrow' was explicitly not a commitment; asserting a firm ship date upgrades the hedge.

mustNotContain.needle: done by tomorrow.$|will be done by tomorrow
mustNotContain.why: Lee said don't hold him to tomorrow; a flat commitment is a fabrication.

lowConfidence.why: Dashboard tool name came through garbled ('Graphana'); should be cited as heard if not confidently resolved.

speakerChecks.claim: your deploy broke staging with 500s
the config change / deploy that broke staging
prod payment provider hopefully done by tomorrow

title: 1:1 short — end handoff + hedge
transcript:
Tara (00:00)
Hey Nate, thanks for hopping on. Just our regular one-on-one. How's the week going?

Nate (00:06)
Pretty good. The onboarding flow work is basically wrapped. I'm mostly cleaning up edge cases now.

Tara (00:13)
Nice. How are you feeling about the load overall? Not too crushed?

Nate (00:18)
Honestly it's manageable. The one thing on my mind is the auth module. It's gotten crufty and every change is scary. I might refactor the auth module while it's fresh in my head, but I'm not sure yet — it's a big chunk of work and I don't want to derail the onboarding cleanup. So it's a maybe, I haven't decided.

Tara (00:37)
That's fair. I don't want you to bite off the auth refactor if it risks the onboarding deadline. Keep it as something to consider, not a commitment. If you do decide to take it on, loop me in first so we can size it properly.

Nate (00:50)
Yeah. It's genuinely a maybe. I'll flag it if I decide to actually do it.

Tara (00:55)
Good. Let's talk growth stuff for a sec. You mentioned wanting more backend ownership — the auth module could actually be a good vehicle for that, if you scope it right. But no pressure.

Nate (01:07)
I'd like that eventually. Let me get onboarding fully closed first.

Tara (01:11)
Makes sense. Anything you need from me? Any blockers?

Nate (01:15)
Not really. The one thing — the vendor call about the new email provider. You've been running that. Where does it stand?

Tara (01:23)
Right, good that you raised it, because this ties into something I need to tell you. The vendor evaluation is mid-flight — we've got one more call with them to nail down pricing and the migration timeline. And here's the thing I need to hand off. I'm going to be out for two weeks starting Monday — I'm taking leave for a family thing, fully offline.

Nate (01:44)
Oh, okay. Two weeks starting Monday.

Tara (01:47)
Right. So while I'm out, a few things can't just sit. The big one is that vendor call — it's scheduled for Wednesday, right in the middle of my leave, and it can't slip because their quote expires. So I need you to cover the vendor call while I'm out. You've been close enough to it.

Nate (02:04)
Okay, I can take the vendor call Wednesday.

Tara (02:07)
Thank you. And anything that comes up that would normally come to me — launch blockers, urgent decisions — don't sit on them waiting for me to come back. Take them to Priya. She's covering for me while I'm on leave. So the coverage is: Priya for anything urgent, and you specifically own the vendor call on Wednesday.

Nate (02:26)
Got it. Vendor call is mine Wednesday, everything else urgent goes to Priya while you're out.

Tara (02:31)
Exactly. And the timing matters — I'm gone Monday, so anything you need from me, grab it before Friday, because after that I'm unreachable for two weeks. If you want my read on the auth refactor idea, ask me before Friday, else it waits till I'm back.

Nate (02:46)
Understood. I'll get any questions to you before Friday.

Tara (02:49)
Perfect. Have a good rest of the week, Nate.

mustContain.why: Nate's auth refactor is an explicit maybe; the hedge must survive as proposed, not owned.
Reinforces that the auth refactor was not committed.

mustNotContain.needle: will refactor the auth|Nate will refactor|refactoring the auth module this
mustNotContain.why: Nate said 'might refactor... not sure yet'; a flat commitment upgrades the hedge.
Ownership of the auth refactor was explicitly not accepted.

continuity.why: Tara's two-week leave starting Monday, planted at the end, must reach continuity.
Priya covers anything urgent while Tara is out — the coverage handoff.
Nate specifically owns the Wednesday vendor call during Tara's absence — a handoff with timing.

speakerChecks.claim: out for two weeks starting Monday on leave
might refactor the auth module, not sure yet
cover the vendor call on Wednesday
